title: Pierogi with potato and cheese
subtitle: Polish stuffed dumplings with mashed potato and cheddar filling.
excerpt: A tender egg and soured cream dough folded around a filling of buttery mashed potato and sharp cheddar, boiled until they float, then panfried in butter until golden. Makes about 30 pierogi, serving four.
sourceNotes: Polish pierogi. Stuffed dumplings are documented across Central and Eastern European cooking from the medieval period. Pierogi appear in Polish cookbooks from the 17th century onward. The potato and cheese filling (ruskie or 'Ruthenian' style) is the most widely eaten filling in Poland. The dish shares family resemblances with Czech knedlíky, Ukrainian varenyky, and Georgian khinkali.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked on a lined tray, then transfer to a bag. Boil from frozen, adding 3 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a double batch and freeze half. Pierogi are ideal batch-cooking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough and filling can both be made the day before and refrigerated separately.

content.text: Pierogi is an assembly project. The dough is easy and forgiving; the filling is just mashed potato with cheese. The work is in the making: rolling, cutting, filling, sealing. Allow an hour at the table, ideally with company. The reward is a dinner that the whole house will eat with enthusiasm.

items.prepNote: finely diced, cooked in butter until golden

content.text: Mix {{plain-flour}} of flour, {{eggs}} egg, {{soured-cream}} of soured cream and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt into a dough. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes. For the filling, mash {{potato}} of boiled potato until smooth. Cook {{onion}} diced onion in {{unsalted-butter}} of butter until golden; stir into the potato with {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. The filling should be dry enough to hold its shape.

Roll the dough to 2 to 3 mm thick. Cut circles about 8 cm across using a glass or cutter. Place a teaspoon of filling in the centre of each circle. Wet the edges with water, fold the dough over the filling to form a half-moon, and press the edges firmly to seal. Crimp with a fork.

Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Cook pierogi in batches; they are done 2 minutes after they float to the surface. Drain. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the boiled pierogi and fry for 2 to 3 minutes per side until golden. Serve with soured cream.

items.cause: Seams not pressed firmly enough, or too much filling.
items.fix: Press the edges firmly and double-crimp with a fork. Use a level teaspoon of filling; overfilling is the most common cause of bursting.

items.cause: Too little flour, or dough not rested.
items.fix: Rest for the full 30 minutes. Dust the work surface generously; the soured cream in the dough keeps it moist.

items.cause: Potato not dried after boiling, or too much butter in the filling.
items.fix: Drain and return potato to the hot pan for 2 minutes to evaporate moisture before mashing.

content.text: Pierogi is the comfort food of Poland, the dish that Poles make in large quantities and eat with their families. The potato and cheese filling is the most everyday version; other fillings include sauerkraut and mushroom, meat, and sweet versions with cherry or blueberry. Making pierogi by hand is slow enough to be a communal activity: a table covered in flour, everyone with a task, and a pot of soured cream at the end.
